title: Beads admin: retitle plxd phase 1-3 tasks to chevrond
description:
Quick fixup after rebrand completes:

- plx-1yz: '[epic] plxd: persistent filesystem-watching daemon' → '[epic] chevrond: ...'
- plx-1yz.1: 'plxd phase 1: ...' → 'chevrond phase 1: ...'
- plx-1yz.2: 'plxd phase 3: ...' → 'chevrond phase 3: ...'
- plx-1yz.4: 'plxd phase 2: ...' → 'chevrond phase 2: ...'

Bead IDs (plx-1yz.*) stay as-is — those are stable identifiers, not display names. Only titles change. The IDs may eventually be renamed via 'bd rename-prefix plx chevron' but that's separate cosmetic work.

title: Local rename: Cargo, source, shell init, flake, scripts, docs
description:
The in-tree mechanical changes:

- Cargo.toml: name = 'chevron', repository URL → shiprock/chevron, add dual [[bin]] entries (chevron primary, plx alias). Both point to src/main.rs.
- src/main.rs, benches/segments.rs: 'use plx::' → 'use chevron::'. Verify cargo check + cargo bench --no-run.
- src/shell.rs: init_zsh/init_bash/init_fish output 'chevron <cmd>' calls instead of 'plx <cmd>'. Snapshot tests will catch any miss.
- flake.nix: pname = 'chevron', add postInstall symlink $out/bin/plx → chevron for back-compat. Verify nix build still works.
- scripts/bench.sh: BIN=./target/release/chevron.
- README.md: full rewrite, add 'formerly plx' note, install instructions show 'cargo install chevron' and 'nix run github:shiprock/chevron'.
- CLAUDE.md: rewrite project description, file paths still accurate, mention chevron throughout.
- AGENTS.md: rewrite.
- Health check / weather error messages that say 'plx:' → 'chevron:'.

Acceptance: full test suite passes (lib + integration + benches build), 'plx' compat binary exists and produces identical output to 'chevron', release build runs cleanly.

Does NOT touch GitHub or crates.io — those are separate sub-tasks.

title: GitHub: transfer mmichie/plx to shiprock org, rename to chevron
description:
One-time admin operation via gh CLI (or GitHub web UI):

1. Push any pending work first (ensure local master is clean).
2. Transfer mmichie/plx → shiprock org: 'gh repo transfer mmichie/plx shiprock' (or web UI). Requires confirmation from a shiprock admin.
3. Once transferred, rename: 'gh repo rename --repo shiprock/plx chevron'.
4. GitHub serves 301 redirects from mmichie/plx → shiprock/chevron for both repo URL and git remote.
5. Update local git remote: 'git remote set-url origin git@github.com:shiprock/chevron.git'.
6. Verify: 'gh repo view shiprock/chevron', confirm Actions/issues/PRs migrated.
7. Update Cargo.toml repository URL to the new path (done in plx-7rc.1 if not already).

Done by user — requires shiprock org admin perms and confirmation prompts. Cannot be fully automated by agent.

title: Rebrand plx → chevron, transfer mmichie/plx → shiprock/chevron
description:
First public release under a new name. plx is taken on crates.io (Samuel Roland's learning tool). chevron is available everywhere and is semantically tied to powerline arrows. Move repo to shiprock org as the umbrella brand.

Constraints:
- Binary name 'plx' MUST stay shipped as a compat alias (back-compat for existing users like Pat). The new primary binary is 'chevron'; both point to the same source via dual [[bin]] entries.
- Shell init (src/shell.rs) emits 'chevron' command calls going forward, but old configs running 'plx' still work because the alias binary exists.
- crates.io publish is a one-way door (you can yank but not unpublish) — be sure on the name before first publish.

Order: this must complete BEFORE plxd phase 1 (plx-1yz.1) so the daemon ships as chevrond, not plxd.

See sub-tasks for the breakdown.

title: plxd phase 0: extract RepoStatus from segments::git::render
description:
Pull the data-computation half of segments::git::render into a pure RepoStatus struct + compute(repo) function. Render becomes pure formatting over RepoStatus.

This is the load-bearing refactor for the plxd daemon: it gives the daemon a stable data type to cache and serve, and lets the inline-fallback and daemon paths share identical compute code (the invariant that makes daemon-as-pure-optimization safe).

Independent value beyond the daemon: enables snapshot-style render tests over hand-built RepoStatus values, the way tmux_title::render_from_info works today. Cheaper, more precise, no tempdir+libgit2 per test.

Scope (~200 LoC change):
- New src/repo_status_data.rs (final name TBD — current repo_status.rs is the interactive status view; rename one) holding RepoStatus + compute()
- segments::git::render(status, from_bg) becomes pure formatting; remove the libgit2 calls from render
- Update callers in segments::git::render (top-level) and segments::tmux_title to call compute() then render()
- Reuse GitInfo::gather logic (it already does this split for tmux-title); generalize to cover all fields
- Add snapshot tests for render over fixed RepoStatus inputs

Must not regress the no-daemon perf path — verify with the criterion benches added in plx-32m.
close_reason: phase 0 complete: RepoStatus extracted from segments::git::render; 283 lib tests + 42 integration tests passing; perf neutral-to-improved (render_clean 690→468µs, render_20_untracked 664→548µs, compute_cwd unchanged at ~640µs); snapshot tests cover all 7 canonical render scenarios

title: Fold tmux title into plx prompt
description: Generate tmux title inside plx prompt as second output line when $TMUX is set. Eliminates redundant Repository::discover and reduces tmux IPC calls from 5-6 to 1-2, saving ~36ms per prompt.
close_reason: Implemented two-line output protocol, all 64 tests pass, clippy clean

title: First publish: crates.io 'chevron' + tag release
description:
Once local rename merged and GitHub repo moved:

1. cargo publish --dry-run --allow-dirty to sanity-check the package contents.
2. cargo login (one-time, with crates.io token tied to the publishing identity — recommend personal account first, add shiprock as co-owner after).
3. Bump version: 0.4.0 → 0.5.0 (the rebrand is a breaking change in name, so minor bump is correct under 0.x semver).
4. git tag v0.5.0, git push --tags.
5. cargo publish (no --dry-run). This is a ONE-WAY DOOR — chevron 0.5.0 cannot be unpublished, only yanked.
6. Verify on crates.io that the page renders, README shows, repository link is correct.
7. (Optional) Add shiprock as co-owner: 'cargo owner --add github:shiprock:owners chevron'.

Bonus: open a PR to nixpkgs adding pkgs/by-name/ch/chevron/package.nix that pulls from crates.io. Or defer until plxd phase 1 ships.

title: plxd phase 2: invalidate cache via .git/ filesystem watching
description:
Replace TTL invalidation with notify-crate watchers on the per-repo .git/ control files.

Watched set (small, bounded — avoids inotify watch-count blowup):
- .git/HEAD          (branch name, detached state)
- .git/index         (staged count)
- .git/refs/stash    (stash count)
- .git/refs/heads/*  (ahead/behind via upstream tracking)

These tiny files signal all user-visible state transitions that come from explicit git commands (commit, stage, stash, checkout, branch). Working-tree edits (modified/untracked counts) still require a libgit2 status walk on next query, with a much shorter re-cache window (e.g. 100ms).

Components (~300 LoC):
- src/daemon/watcher.rs using notify crate
- Watcher thread sends Invalidate(repo_path) messages to state thread
- Repo registration: on first STATUS query for an unwatched repo, state thread adds it to the watcher and computes RepoStatus
- Idle eviction: drop repos with no query in 1h
- Memory cap: max N watched repos (LRU eviction)

Aggressive race tests: intentionally race fs events against queries; assert no stale-data window > 100ms after a commit/stage/stash. This is the bug class that would be catastrophically worse UX than today's slow-but-correct prompt.

Blocked by phase 1.

title: plxd phase 1: TTL-cached daemon, sync threads, text protocol, auto-spawn
description:
Build the daemon scaffolding with no filesystem watching yet. Captures ~80% of the perf win for typical interactive use because most prompt redraws happen within ~1s of the prior one.

Components (~600-800 LoC):
- src/daemon/ module, feature = 'daemon' (default-on)
- Actor model: state thread (owns HashMap<PathBuf, RepoStatus>) + listener thread (UnixListener) + 4-thread handler pool. All comms via std::sync::mpsc. NO tokio.
- Text protocol with HELLO version handshake, STATUS opcode, QUIT
- Socket at $XDG_RUNTIME_DIR/plxd.sock (Linux) / /tmp/plxd-$UID/sock (macOS), mode 0700
- Auto-spawn from client on connect-fail (fork+exec self with --daemonize, double-fork+setsid, flock lockfile dedup)
- Client-side: 2ms connect timeout, 5ms total query budget, fall back to inline compute() on any failure or timeout
- 1-second TTL cache per repo path
- plx daemon {start|stop|status|logs} subcommands
- Logging to $XDG_STATE_HOME/plxd.log with simple rotation

Tests:
- Unit: state thread loop with channel-driven Query/Invalidate
- Protocol: text encode/decode round-trip
- E2E: spawn daemon as subprocess in tempdir, run plx git against tempdir-cwd, kill daemon mid-test to exercise fallback
- Invariant: inline compute() == daemon-served RepoStatus for the same fixture repo

Blocked by phase 0.

title: plxd phase 3: core.fsmonitor protocol for large monorepos
description:
Implement git's core.fsmonitor IPC so git itself queries plxd for changed paths instead of walking the full index. Targets repos in the 100k+ file range (chromium, nixpkgs, linux kernel) where libgit2 status alone is >100ms.

Most users won't need this — moderate repos (plx-sized, <10k files) are already fast enough after phase 2. So this is the last phase and entirely optional.

Components (~500 LoC + protocol study):
- Implement git fsmonitor protocol (V2 query format: client sends token + timestamp, daemon returns changed paths)
- Wire plxd to advertise itself as a fsmonitor: user sets 'git config core.fsmonitor plxd-fsmonitor' or a wrapper script
- Translate notify events into the fsmonitor change-set representation
- May require migrating to gitoxide for the daemon side if libgit2/git2's fsmonitor support is insufficient (libgit2 fsmonitor support was added in 1.5 — verify g2 0.20 exposes it)

Acceptance: 'git status' inside a 100k-file repo with plxd running drops to <20ms (vs >100ms without).

Blocked by phase 2.

title: benches harness: criterion + hyperfine for prompt segments
description: Set up an in-process criterion bench suite for the hot segments (path, git GitInfo::gather, git render, tmux_title, prompt) plus a scripts/bench.sh that runs hyperfine end-to-end against the release binary. Goal: be able to A/B perf changes (e.g. narrowing StatusOptions, folding marker-file scans) with a single command.

title: Adaptive context-aware segment display
description:
Show prompt segments situationally based on current context rather than a fixed layout.

## Motivation
Current prompts show a fixed set of segments regardless of context. A prompt that adapts to what you are doing shows more relevant info with less noise.

## Design
- In a git repo: show git info. Outside: omit entirely (no empty segment).
- In a nix shell: show nix. Otherwise: nothing (already done).
- Long-running command just finished (>2s): show duration prominently. Otherwise: hide.
- Deep in a monorepo: show path relative to repo root, not home.
- In a Python venv: show venv name. In node project: show node version. Etc.
- Plugin/situations directory where each situation is a small check+render unit

## Key decisions
- Detection logic per-segment (like Starship) vs centralized situation resolver
- How to handle the daemon case (daemon tracks project type too?)
- Balance between smart defaults and configurability

## Acceptance criteria
- cd into a git repo shows git segment, cd out hides it (no empty space)
- Monorepo paths show repo-relative path when deep
- No segment renders empty brackets or placeholder when not relevant
- Plugin interface documented for adding new situations

title: Emit OSC 133 semantic terminal markers
description:
Add OSC 133 escape sequences to mark prompt boundaries so modern terminals (Ghostty, WezTerm, iTerm2, Kitty) can provide enhanced navigation.

## Motivation
OSC 133 semantic zones let terminals jump between prompts, select entire command output with one click, and correctly reflow prompts on resize. Almost no prompt tool emits these today — easy win with outsized UX impact.

## Design
- Emit OSC 133 A (prompt start) before first segment
- Emit OSC 133 B (prompt end / input start) after last segment
- Shell hooks emit OSC 133 C (input end / output start) on preexec
- Shell hooks emit OSC 133 D (output end) with exit code on precmd

## Key decisions
- Can be implemented standalone (just markers around existing output) or as part of shell integration epic
- Should detect terminal capability and skip markers on dumb terminals
- Markers are invisible — zero visual impact, pure metadata

## Acceptance criteria
- Ghostty cmd-click on prompt jumps to previous/next prompt
- iTerm2 command history navigation works
- No visual artifacts on terminals that don't support OSC 133
- Works with both Starship custom command mode and native shell hooks

title: Async/transient prompt via shell integration
description:
Ship shell hooks (zsh/bash/fish) that enable async prompt rendering and transient prompt collapsing.

## Motivation
Current model: Starship forks plx as a subprocess, blocks until done. Even with the daemon, there is fork overhead. Shell hooks eliminate this entirely and enable UX that subprocess-based prompts cannot do.

## Design
- Shell hooks (precmd/preexec) that query plxd or run plx inline
- Display cached/stale prompt instantly, update asynchronously when fresh data arrives
- Stale data rendered in dim color, replaced with bright when ready
- Transient prompt: on Enter, collapse previous prompt to minimal (e.g. just dollar sign) for clean scrollback and easy copy-paste
- Post-execution update: after command finishes, rewrite prompt with duration and exit code

## Key decisions
- Support zsh first (precmd/preexec), then bash (PROMPT_COMMAND), then fish
- Can coexist with Starship or replace it entirely
- Transient prompt requires terminal support for cursor movement (widely supported)

## Acceptance criteria
- Prompt appears in <5ms regardless of git state complexity
- Stale data updates within 100ms of daemon response
- Previous prompts collapse to single character in scrollback
- Works in zsh and bash

title: plxd: persistent filesystem-watching daemon
description:
Build a background daemon that maintains hot git/project state in memory, queried over Unix socket for <1ms prompt responses.

## Motivation
Every prompt tool today recomputes everything from scratch on every Enter. Powerlevel10k's gitstatusd proved a daemon architecture is 10-40x faster, but it's now unmaintained and zsh-only.

## Design
- Long-lived Rust process watching filesystem via macOS FSEvents / Linux inotify
- Integrates with git core.fsmonitor for efficient dirty checking
- Maintains repo state (branch, staged, modified, untracked, stash, ahead/behind) always warm
- Serves queries over Unix socket
- Graceful fallback: if daemon not running, compute inline like today

## Key decisions
- Use gitoxide instead of git2 for the daemon (better async support)
- Socket protocol: simple binary or JSON request/response
- Lifecycle: auto-start on first prompt query, idle timeout shutdown
- Multi-repo support: track multiple repos simultaneously

## Acceptance criteria
- plx git queries daemon when available, falls back to direct git2
- Prompt latency <5ms with daemon vs current 50-500ms
- Daemon reacts to filesystem changes within 100ms
- Works on macOS and Linux
design:
## Architectural primitive: separate compute from render

Pull the data-computation half of `segments::git::render` into a pure
`RepoStatus` struct + `compute(repo: &Repository) -> RepoStatus` function.
`render(status: &RepoStatus, from_bg) -> String` becomes pure formatting.

The daemon becomes a cache over `compute`. Both inline-fallback and daemon
paths call identical `compute` code — daemon is an optimization, never a
dependency. This invariant is enforced by a test: inline-compute vs
daemon-served must produce identical RepoStatus for identical state.

`GitInfo::gather` is the prototype — it already does this split for the
tmux-title path. Generalize.

## Concurrency: actor model, NO async runtime

- Watcher thread (notify crate) → Invalidate(path) messages
- State thread owns HashMap<PathBuf, RepoStatus>, processes serially
- Listener thread (UnixListener) + 4-thread handler pool
- All inter-thread comms via std::sync::mpsc

Why sync/no tokio: prompt workloads are low-QPS, tokio's startup cost is
non-trivial, and the actor model gives clean panic isolation + trivial
unit tests (drive state thread by channel messages, no I/O needed).

## Protocol: line-oriented text over UDS

Socket: $XDG_RUNTIME_DIR/plxd.sock (Linux) or /tmp/plxd-$UID/sock (macOS),
mode 0700.

```
> HELLO 1
< HELLO 1
> STATUS /path
< OK branch=... staged=N modified=N untracked=N ...
> QUIT
```

Why text: debuggable with `socat`, trivial protocol code, schema evolves
by adding keys. Parse cost is <1µs vs ~200µs socket round-trip.

Stateless beyond HELLO. No streams, no subscriptions.

## Lifecycle: client auto-spawns, never depends

- Client connect with 2ms timeout, query with 5ms total budget
- On connect-fail: fall back to inline `compute` AND fork+exec self as
  daemon (don't wait for it; next render uses the warm daemon)
- flock on lockfile to dedup simultaneous spawns (12 shells → 1 daemon)
- Double-fork + setsid for detachment

## Module layout

```
src/
  repo_status_data.rs  # NEW: RepoStatus struct + compute()
  segments/git.rs       # pure render() over RepoStatus
  daemon/               # feature = "daemon", default-on
    mod.rs proto.rs state.rs watcher.rs listener.rs
    lifecycle.rs client.rs
```

Client mode never executes daemon code paths. `--no-default-features
--features=core,banner,weather` produces a daemon-less embedded build.

## Anti-patterns to refuse

- Arc<Mutex<HashMap>> shared across threads (use channels)
- Loading TOML config in the daemon (render lives in client only)
- Streaming/subscription opcodes (stateless request/response only)
- Shelling out to `git` from the daemon (libgit2/gitoxide only)
- Persisting cache across daemon restarts (in-memory only)
- Async I/O for the socket (sync threads are simpler at this scale)
- Letting client-side fallback drift from daemon output (invariant test)

## Phased rollout

Children: phase 0 → phase 1 → phase 2 → phase 3, sequential.

Phase 0 is independently valuable (cleaner tests) and unblocks all later
phases. Phase 1 captures ~80% of the perf win with TTL caching and no FS
watcher. Each phase is strictly additive — earlier phase's code keeps
working if a later phase is disabled.
